name: Río Pudio Green Corridor Loop
name_original: Corredor Verde del Río Pudio – circular desde  San Juan de Aznalfarache
description: Cycle the Green Corridor of the Rio Pudio, a moderate 24.7-mile route from San Juan de Aznalfarache, featuring river views and historic sigh
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around San Juan De Aznalfarache are situated on the Aljarafe plateau, offering diverse terrain overlooking the Guadalquivir River. The region features flat paths along the Guadalquivir River Walk and green corridors like the Río Pudio, alongside agricultural landscapes with traditional olive groves. Its highest point, the Cerro de los Sagrados Corazones, provides panoramic views of the river and Seville. This area combines accessible riverside trails with varied terrain on the plateau. Best touring cycling routes around San Juan De Aznalfarache The most popular touring cycling route is Río Pudio Green Corridor Loop, a 24.7 miles (39.8 km) trail that takes 2 hours 33 minutes to complete. This route offers scenic river views through a dedicated green corridor. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Mairena–Polígono Pisa Footbridge – Cafeteria Camping Dehesa Nueva loop from San Juan Alto, a moderate 31.7 miles (51.0 km) path. This route connects to neighboring towns, traversing varied terrain across the Aljarafe region. Local touring cyclists also love the San Juan Bridge – Puente de las Delicias loop from San Juan Alto, a 38.6 miles (62.1 km) trail leading through urban and natural landscapes with views of the Guadalquivir River, often completed in about 4 hours 23 minutes. Touring cycling around San Juan De Aznalfarache is defined by the Guadalquivir River, the Aljarafe plateau, and green corridors. The network offers options for different ability levels, from flat riverside paths to routes with moderate elevation changes. The routes in San Juan De Aznalfarache are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 80 reviews. More than 900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore San Juan De Aznalfarache's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in San Juan De Aznalfarache?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes around San Juan De Aznalfarache, catering to various skill levels. You'll find 2 easy routes, 25 moderate routes, and 8 difficult routes to explore.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in San Juan De Aznalfarache?
A: The routes offer a diverse mix of terrain. You can enjoy flat paths along the Guadalquivir River Walk, scenic green corridors like the Río Pudio, and varied landscapes across the Aljarafe plateau, which features traditional olive groves. The region is known for its blend of riverside trails and routes with moderate elevation changes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, San Juan De Aznalfarache offers routes suitable for families. The flat paths along the Guadalquivir River Walk and sections of the green corridors provide easier, more relaxed cycling experiences. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988481624/rio-pudio-green-corridor-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Pudio Green Corridor Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers scenic river views and is generally considered moderate, but sections can be enjoyed by families.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints I can see while cycling?
A: San Juan De Aznalfarache, known as the 'Balcony of Seville,' offers unique panoramic views from its highest point, the Cerro de los Sagrados Corazones, overlooking the Guadalquivir River and Seville. Along your rides, you can also incorporate cultural exploration by visiting historical sites like the Almohad Walls. For routes extending into Seville, you might pass by iconic landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/215404" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seville Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/112737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza of Spain&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tower of Gold&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in San Juan De Aznalfarache?
A: The region enjoys a pleasant climate year-round, making it conducive to outdoor activities. Spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for cycling. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended, while winters are mild and often sunny.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988481624/rio-pudio-green-corridor-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Pudio Green Corridor Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 39.8 km (24.7 miles) trail offering scenic river views. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38267202" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Bridge – Puente de las Delicias loop from San Juan Alto&lt;/a&gt;, a 62.1 km (38.6 miles) route through urban and natural landscapes.

Q: Can I access these cycling routes using public transport?
A: San Juan De Aznalfarache is well-connected within the Seville metropolitan area. Its close proximity to Seville means you can often access routes that connect to the city's extensive bicycle-friendly network. Public transport options like the metro or buses can help you reach starting points for many routes, especially those connecting to Seville.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in San Juan De Aznalfarache?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 80 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from flat riverside paths to routes with moderate elevation changes, and the beautiful views of the Guadalquivir River and Seville. The accessibility to green corridors and connections to neighboring towns are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any longer or more challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a longer and more challenging ride, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38191890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Bridge – Amphitheatre of Italica loop from San Juan Alto&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 89.0 km (55.3 miles) route. This trail offers significant elevation changes and takes you through varied landscapes, including historical sites like the Amphitheatre of Italica.

Q: Are there options for connecting to other towns or regions by bike?
A: Yes, San Juan De Aznalfarache serves as a hub for cycling tours that connect to neighboring towns within the Aljarafe region, such as Tomares, Mairena del Aljarafe, and Coria del Río. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19347347" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mairena–Polígono Pisa Footbridge – Cafeteria Camping Dehesa Nueva loop from San Juan Alto&lt;/a&gt; traverse varied terrain across the Aljarafe, linking different communities.

Q: Where can I find parking near the touring cycling routes?
A: Parking is generally available in and around San Juan De Aznalfarache, especially near residential areas or public spaces. For specific trailheads, it's advisable to check local signage. Many routes start from accessible points within the town or nearby, making it convenient to find parking before beginning your ride.

Q: Are there any specific green corridors ideal for cycling?
A: Absolutely. The Río Pudio Green Corridor is a significant feature for cyclists, with a dedicated loop starting directly from San Juan De Aznalfarache. This corridor offers scenic river views and a pleasant cycling environment. Additionally, the Guadiamar Green Corridor, a protected natural area, is accessible from nearby Aznalcázar and provides routes through forests.
tips: Corredor verde del Aljarafe is one of the few natural areas close to Seville - a ~20 km "green lung" along the banks of the Riopudio stream. The name "Pudio" of the river comes from the Latin word "putidus" - putrefied, because in Roman times the river passed through sulfur deposits that gave it this characteristic smell.
Remodeled bridge in 2002 that crosses the Pudio river. There is a wooded area with picnic areas and, very close, a lot of fig trees that give away delicious brevas and figs every year.
A small artificial waterfall on the Pudio River. Good place to rest for a while.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is approximately 40 km long and generally takes around 2.5 to 3 hours to complete by touring bicycle, depending on your pace and stops. It's rated as moderate, so factor in time for the varied terrain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Green Corridor of the Rio Pudio?
faqs.answer: The route features a varied terrain. You'll encounter significant sections of compacted gravel (about 16.5 km), paved surfaces (around 14.3 km), and asphalt (about 5.7 km). There are also some shorter unpaved segments (around 3.15 km) that might be a bit more challenging. Overall, it's a mix of cycleways, streets, roads, and some singletrack.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: While parts of the broader Corredor Verde del Riopudio are known for being easy and flat, this specific circular route from San Juan de Aznalfarache is rated as 'moderate' and requires 'good fitness'. It includes an elevation gain of approximately 250 meters and varied terrain, so it might be challenging for absolute beginners. Cyclists with some experience will find it more enjoyable.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or features will I see on the route?
faqs.answer: As you cycle, you'll follow the Rio Pudio, enjoying scenery with water and vegetation. Key highlights include the Green Corridor of the Pudio River itself, the historic Roman Bridge Over the Pudio River, and the scenic Río Pudio Waterfall. You'll also pass through or near several municipalities in the Aljarafe region, offering glimpses of towns and villages.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot in Seville, making cycling less comfortable, while winters are generally mild but can have more rainfall.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this circular route in San Juan de Aznalfarache?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, San Juan de Aznalfarache is a municipality, so you should be able to find public parking within the town to access the trailhead. Look for designated parking zones or street parking near the start of the route.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is part of the larger Corredor Verde del Riopudio network. It also intersects with sections of the Cañada Real de las Islas, Corredor Verde de Sevilla, and the Carril bici Antonio Navarro - Bormujos, offering opportunities to explore further if you wish.

faqs.question: Is the Green Corridor of the Rio Pudio dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, as a Vía Verde, the Green Corridor is generally accessible for pedestrians and cyclists, and often for pets. However, always keep your dog on a leash to ensure their safety and respect other users of the path, especially in sections that might be shared with other cyclists or pedestrians.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Green Corridor of the Rio Pudio. It's a public greenway, part of Spain's Vías Verdes network, designed for free public access.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route traverses various municipalities within the Aljarafe region, including Bollullos, Olivares, Villanueva del Ariscal, Salteras, Espartinas, Bormujos, and Mairena del Aljarafe. These towns and villages are often visible from the route and provide opportunities to stop for refreshments, food, or other services.
